EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system and cold front moved through southern New England on Friday bringing a 4-8 hour period of strong winds which caused sporadic damage in CT. Winds generally gusted 50-60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds generally gusted 40-50 mph but some gusted higher including a 64 mph gust from the ASOS at Willimantic Airport at 11:03 AM EST. In Plainfield, downed trees and wires closed Squaw Rock Rd. In Putnam a tree came down onto a house. In Moosup downed trees and wires closed Pond Hill Rd. In Pomfret a tree dame down on a car.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe Drought (D2) conditions continued across northwestern Connecticut, per the U.S. Drought Monitor, into the beginning of March before periods of new precipitation led to a downgrade to Moderate Drought (D1) conditions on March 11. The Stage 2 drought advisory, per the Connecticut Interagency Drought Workgroup, continued through this period. At this stage, residents and businesses were encouraged to minimize unnecessary water usage.

The pressure gradient tightened as a cold front approached from the west. This produced strong winds over Southern Connecticut during the evening of March 5, 2025.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media reported power lines downed near the Grovers Avenue vicinity in Bridgeport, CT at around 745 PM Local Standard Time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abnormally dry conditions across portions of northwestern Connecticut resulted in the development of severe drought conditions (Severe - D2) per the U.S. Drought Monitor across portions of Litchfield County starting on November 12, 2024 and continued into early January 2025. On November 9, 2024, Governor Ned Lamont, per the recommendation of the Connecticut Interagency Drought Workgroup, declared a Stage 2 drought advisory for all of Connecticut. At this stage, residents and businesses were encouraged to minimize unnecessary water usage. The drought status was temporarily improved on January 7, 2025 (Moderate - D1), per the U.S. Drought Monitor and continued through the rest of the month. The Stage 2 drought advisory, per the Connecticut Interagency Drought Workgroup, continued through the month of January.

A passing cold front moves through southern New England bringing a line of storms which produced relatively widespread damaging winds, taking down trees and power lines in western/central MA and CT. Storms fizzled out as they reached RI and southeast MA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Granby, multiple trees were down on power lines on Buttles Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure and cold front passed through the area during the late afternoon to evening hours of February 16, 2025. A tight pressure gradient behind the passage resulted in high winds across Southern Connecticut from the evening hours of February 16 through the morning hours of February 17, 2025.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 657 AM Local Standard Time, a measured high wind gust of 63 mph occurred at the Norwalk Light mesonet station located less than 1 mile southeast of Calf Pasture Point in Norwalk, CT.

The first instance occurred at 427 PM Local Standard Time as a tree was downed, closing Route 188 between Routes 34 and 344 in Seymour, CT. The second instance involved large tree branches downed on Route 42 in Cheshire, CT between Inverness Court and Candee Road at 818 PM Local Standard Time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest Connecticut was impacted by an arctic airmass from January 21-22, 2025. The combination of below normal air temperatures, breezy winds, and nighttime radiational cooling led to minimum wind chill (or feels'-like temperatures) between -10 and -20 degrees Fahrenheit in the early morning hours on January 21 and January 22. The lowest of these air temperatures and wind chill values occurred at the highest elevations of the Litchfield Hills.

An area of low pressure tracking from the Carolinas northeastward off the East Coast brought a widespread light to moderate snowfall to northwestern Massachusetts on January 19-20, 2025. Snowfall amounts were generally between 3 and 5 inches resulting in slippery travel conditions.||Key impacts: travel delays.

A negatively tilted upper level trough approached aloft while an associated surface low's triple point passed just north of the area. Severe thunderstorms formed along the cold front and produced damaging wind gusts the night of New Year's Eve into the early morning of New Year's Day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet site in Old Saybrook measured a 51 knot wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Windy conditions occurred across portions of northwestern Connecticut with peak wind gusts between 40 and 50 mph on January 7 and January 9. These winds resulted in localized downed tree limbs.

A cold front pushed through, with a secondary cold frontal boundary lingering back to the west. Low pressure formed to the south along the boundary and began to strengthen as it pulled away quickly to the east and northeast. The accumulating snow was generated mainly from frontogenetical forcing which occurred to the north and west of the developing surface low. Winter Storm criteria was just missed across No. Fairfield with the majority of the remainder of Southern Connecticut reaching advisory level snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported 5.5 inches of snow at 4 NNW New Fairfield, 5.2 inches in New Fairfield, 4.3 inches in Danbury, and 3.8 inches in Weston.  Cocorahs reported 4.5 inches in Bethel, 4.3 inches at 2.4 NNE Ridgefield and 4.0 inches in Brookfield.  Snow reports were at advisory level criteria.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system brought a period of snow changing to a wintry mix from south to north across northwest Connecticut during the morning hours on February 6, 2025. Snowfall amounts generally ranged from a coating to 2 inches with a light glaze of ice resulting in slippery travel conditions, especially during the morning commute.||Key impacts: travel delays.

Low pressure lifted northeast out of the Tennessee Valley and tracked just west of the area with the warm front getting up against south and southeastern portions of the region. A light accumulating snow changed to a prolonged period of freezing rain across northern interior sections. The greatest impact was further north, especially along and just south of the I 84 corridor with a fair amount of power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Meriden ASOS reported 0.54 inches of ice, Broadcast media at Hamden reported 0.25 inches of ice, and a trained spotter reported 0.25 inches of ice at North Haven. Tree branches and wires were reported down in Hamden, with large branches reported down in West Haven. Route 243 was closed between Baldwin  Road and Wepawaug Road because of wires down.   Generally 1 to 2 inches of snow fell before the changeover to freezing rain across the zone.  Trained spotters reported 2.0 inches at 1 NNW North Haven and at 1 ENE North Haven, 2.0 inches at Meriden, and 1.8 inches 2 NW Waterbury. Utility company (Eversource) reported at least 15,400 customers were without power, with nearly 1,500 of those located in Newtown, with nearly 1,200 United Illuminating customers as of 24 hours after the storm. By 42 hours after the storm on the following Monday morning Eversource upped the number to 85,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong, westerly winds developed across northwest Connecticut behind the passage of a cold front as amplifying surface high pressure approached from the Ohio Valley and a low pressure system strengthened east of the New England Coast. Peak wind gusts across Litchfield County ranged from around 30 to 40 miles per hour.

A progressive low pressure system emerged out of the Tennessee Valley and quickly moved across the region bringing an accumulating wet snowfall. Most places received an advisory level snowfall with mainly 3 to 4 inches of snow, with some locations getting slightly higher and lower amounts. This was a front end quick thump driven by warm advection snowfall which fell primarily during the evening hours of the 8th. The snow quickly tapered to some light freezing rain and drizzle in spots after the initial thump, and changed back to a lighter snow before ending in the early morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Accumulating snow fell across the zone mostly in a 4 to 5 hour period, mainly during the late evening across the zone, with brief mixed precip and lighter snow afterwards towards early morning. Cocorahs observers reported 6.1 inches in Sandy Hook, 5.0 inches at 5.3 S Newtown, 4.7 inches in Bethel, and 4.8 inches in Ridgefield. Trained spotters reported 5.0 inches at 1 E Danbury and at 5 ENE Redding, along with 4.2 inches at 4 NNW New Fairfield. Danbury ASOS reported 0.02 inches of freezing rain.

Everclear utility reported thousand of power outages in the Higganum area. Power lines were reported down off Route 154 in Haddam. And based on radar a large swath of the northern half of Northern Middlesex would have experienced around a half inch of ice. 1 to 3 inches of snow fell before the changeover to freezing rain. Due to impacts and radar estimation of QPF while temperatures were at or below freezing, winter storm / ice warning criteria is estimated to have been reached. Trained spotters reported 2.8 at 4 W Cromwell, 2.3 inches at Higganum, 2.0 inches at Middletown, and 1.5 inches in Durham.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe discrete thunderstorms were initiated along a slow moving frontal boundary in an area with about 1000 J/kg of SBCAPE. They were able to become somewhat organized given the 40 kt of effective bulk shear that was available due to a belt of southwesterly flow aloft. The storms produced damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree limbs and powerlines down near Ridgefield CT.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Burlington there were multiple trees and  wires down.

A slow moving cold front was the causing factor of showers and early afternoon thunderstorms across northwest Connecticut on Saturday, May 3, 2025. One linear convective system in particular became severe in strength, inflicting wind damage to trees and powerlines across central Litchfield County. Several reports were conveyed to NWS Albany of downed trees and wires blocking roadways and causing temporary closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public reported multiple downed trees blocking South Main Street, US 7, just south of Kent.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With the area warm sectored, MLCAPE values increased to 500-1000 J/kg through the afternoon. Falling heights aloft from an approaching upper level trough and a cold front at the surface were able to produce enough lift to spark a broken line of showers and thunderstorms. One portion of the line became severe and produced damaging wind gusts in southern CT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eversource reports 75 customers without power near Sherman CT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper-level disturbance, together with a northward-tracking surface low from the Mid-Atlantic region brought light to moderate snowfall to northwest Connecticut from late April 11 through early afternoon April 12. The heaviest snowfall was concentrated in the higher elevation areas of northern Litchfield County. Accumulations of one half inch to 4 inches were common throughout Litchfield County. As this occurred over a weekend, little impact to travel occurred, though slippery road conditions did occur.||Key impacts: slippery road conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Zone average snowfall of 2.6 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Elevated levels of heat and humidity preceded a potent upper level shortwave and corresponding jet streak and strong cold front that led to the development of scattered showers and thunderstorms across northwest Connecticut. A few of these thunderstorms reached severe magnitude, producing damaging wind gusts across portions of Litchfield County. Most of the damage reports came from within Terryville and described downed trees that lead to blockages and subsequent closures of several roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local 911 call center operator reported a tree down near Allen Street and East Washington Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dome of high pressure brought a period of very hot and humid conditions across northwestern Connecticut from June 22-25, 2025. Peak heat index values (or feels-like temperatures) were between 95 and 104 degrees Fahrenheit in many locations. The highest heat index values (feels-like temperatures) occurred on June 23 with values between 105 and 110 degrees Fahrenheit. Preliminary data from the U.S. Health and Human Services showed that eight-year records for one day heat-related emergency department visits were set during this heat wave across region 1 which included all of Connecticut and the rest of New England. This period of hot weather also prompted the Connecticut Governor to activate the state's extreme hot weather protocol.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Immediately on the heels of a complex winter storm that impacted northwest Connecticut on February 15-16th, 2025, strong winds and bitter cold broke out across Litchfield County. Strongest wind gusts ranged from around 40 mph to 45 mph. The cold airmass inflicted across Litchfield County on the departing side of the storm of the days prior led to wind chill values anywhere from -1 to -5.||Key Impacts: power outages.

A mid level shortwave and surface cold front brough a smattering of strong to severe storms to southern New England in the evening and early overnight hours. This resulted in scattered wind damage reports in central/northeast CT, northern RI, and central MA. It also resulted in some flash flooding in southeast MA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Farmington, strong wind gusts resulted in a few downed trees and large branches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex winter storm impacted northwest Connecticut between February 15th and 16th, 2025 bringing snow, freezing rain, and strong winds. Snow accumulations ranged from about 3 to 4 inches across Litchfield County with flat ice accreting up to 0.3 to 0.35 inches. By the event's conclusion, the total liquid equivalent across Litchfield County ranged from nearly 1.25 to 1.5 inches. The combination of heavy wet snow, heavy ice, and gusty winds led to significant power outages across the region, in addition to numerous motor vehicle accidents, road closures, and subsequent travel delays. ||Key Impacts: power outages, motor vehicle accidents, travel delays, road closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mixed precipitation event led to the combination of zone average snowfall of 3.9 inches, zone average flat ice accumulation of 0.30 inches, and zone average liquid equivalent of 1.25 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough and associated cold front approached the area. Ahead of these features, the area destabilized to about 2000 J/kg of MLCAPE. Storms fired up along a pre-frontal trough and with the help of about 30 knots of Effective Bulk Shear several storms were able to become severe and produce damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree down near Mansion road blocking an entrance to Heritage Woods.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep layered troughing approached the area from the west along with a cold front at the surface. The area was able to destabilize ahead of the cold front to between 1000 and 1500 j/kg of MLCAPE. The front sparked showers and thunderstorms and with 30 to 40 kt of Effective Bulk Shear available, many of the storms became strong to severe. Single cells and multi-cell clusters were able to produce damaging wind gusts in southern CT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree and power lines down on Merrimac Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep layered troughing approached the area from the west along with a cold front at the surface. The area was able to destabilize ahead of the cold front to between 1000 and 1500 j/kg of MLCAPE. The front sparked showers and thunderstorms and with 30 to 40 kt of Effective Bulk Shear available, many of the storms became strong to severe. Single cells and multi-cell clusters were able to produce damaging wind gusts in southern CT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree down on vehicle with occupants inside on White Plains Road at Merritt Parkway. One  injury reported.

One tree fell onto an occupied vehicle resulting in an injury (direct).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm and humid conditions within a low pressure system resulted in heat index values (feels-like temperatures) reaching 95 to 100 degrees across southern portions of Litchfield County on July 1, 2025.

Although shear was overall weak, lift from an approaching pre-frontal trough and upper level trough combined with 1000-2000 J/kg of MLCAPE was enough for a few severe thunderstorms to develop and produce damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: DOT reports tree down on RT 188 at Wheeler Road. Also a few public reports of downed  trees around the town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot and humid conditions ahead of an upper level disturbance and surface boundary generated enough instability to drive the development of scattered thunderstorms across northwest Connecticut on the afternoon of August 13, 2025.  One storm in particular reached severe magnitude, inflicting wind damage to wires near Sharon which ended up causing a brush fire on Cornwall Bridge Road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An amateur radio operator reported wires down causing a brush fire on Cornwall  Bridge Road in Sharon, Connecticut.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system and cold front moved through southern New England on Friday bringing a 4-8 hour period of strong winds which caused sporadic damage in CT. Winds generally gusted 50-60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds generally gusted 40-50 mph but some gusts were higher including a 60 mph gust in Berlin at 12:46 PM EST. In South Windsor a tree came down on wires near the Manchester line. In East Windsor a tree was blocking the north bound lane of Barber Hill Rd. In Manchester power lines were down on Spruce St and West Meadow Turnpike.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved across New Haven County, CT producing 1 inch of rainfall an hour. A total of 4 to 5 inches fell across New Haven.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six inches of running water across I-84 Westbound in Middlebury, CT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex, relatively progressive winter storm impacted northwest Connecticut in the preliminary days of December, bringing widespread light to locally moderate snow across Litchfield County. ||The main precipitation shield then moved in around or just after 8 AM, bringing light to locally moderate snowfall rates at times throughout the day. Snow concluded in the evening of December 2 with total accumulations ranging from about 0.5 to 5 inches. ||Being that this was an early season storm with the greatest accumulation thus far, many accidents were reported with very slick road conditions. ||Key Impacts: Traffic accidents, travel delays.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Zone average snowfall of 3.5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front passed through southern Connecticut during the afternoon of December 19, 2025. The pressure gradient tightened in response, and produced high winds during the morning into early afternoon hours on this day.

This included a gust of 70 mph at station XGLL - Greens Ledge Light at an elevation of 68 feet, approximately 1 mile south of Roton Point in Rowaton at 1011 AM Local Standard Time. Additionally, a gust of 60 mph was recorded by a station in Fairfield at 1055 AM Local Standard Time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front passed through southern Connecticut during the afternoon of December 19, 2025. The pressure gradient tightened in response, and produced high winds during the morning into early afternoon hours on this day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A measured high wind gust of 67 mph occurred at KGON - Groton Airport at 1101 AM Local Standard Time, and an estimated gust of 66 mph was recorded at mesonet station XSTO - Stonington Outer Breakwater 4, approximately 1 mile south of Stonington Point at 1103 AM Local Standard Time. Additionally, the local Department of Highways reported power lines down on Route 156/Shore Road at Four Mile River Road at 1114 AM Local Standard Time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front passed through southern Connecticut during the afternoon of December 19, 2025. The pressure gradient tightened in response, and produced high winds during the morning into early afternoon hours on this day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were multiple reports of downed trees in the area. The local Department of Highways reported a tree down with wires in Colchester, closing Middletown Road between Bigelow Road and Victoria Drive at 1045 AM Local Standard Time. Additionally, amateur radio reported numerous trees down in Salem, including Rathburn Hill Road, Brainerd Road and Parum Road at an estimated time of 1210 PM Local Standard Time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sub-freezing temperatures ahead of an approaching warm front resulted in a period of accumulating freezing rain for all of northwestern Connecticut during the late afternoon and evening hours on December 28, 2025 into the overnight hours on December 29, 2025. As temperatures rose above freezing, precipitation eventually changed to plain rain. Flat ice accumulations of a light glaze to 0.20 inches occurred which resulted in some slippery travel conditions.

As temperatures rose above freezing, precipitation eventually changed to plain rain. Flat ice accumulations of a light glaze to 0.20 inches occurred which resulted in some slippery travel conditions.

Another complex winter storm impacted northwest Connecticut, this time just after Christmas, December 26-27, 2025. After days worth of storm track shifting in the guidance, the resulting more northerly track of the storm brought widespread moderate to heavy snow across Litchfield County where accumulations ranged from about 5 to nearly 11 inches. ||Key impacts: traffic accidents, travel delays.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Zone average snowfall of 7.9 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through Southern New England in the afternoon bringing a round of severe storms which caused a wide swath of wind damage through MA with some in western CT. Some severe hail occurred in northern MA and a single storm dropped 5 tornadoes in central MA which led to widespread tree damage along but limited damage to structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Burlington, two trees were down blocking Sand Bank Hill Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary stalled to the south of the ALY County Warning Area, in tandem with several mid- and upper-level disturbances generated widespread heavy rainfall and isolated thunderstorms across northwest Connecticut on June 6 and 7. On June 6, one thunderstorm reached severe magnitude in Litchfield County, producing isolated tree damage near Kent Road and Warren Hill Road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 911 call center operator reported a tree down near the Kent Rd and Warren Hill Rd  intersection.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough approached from the west along with an associated surface cold front. The environment over the area ahead of the front was able to destabilize with MLCAPE values reaching about 2000 J/kg. This, combined with ample Effective Bulk Shear of about 45 kt, allowed severe thunderstorms to develop as the cold front moved through the area. These storms produced damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eversource reports a power outage due to tree damage on Mill Hill Road S.
